ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for cassia seed oolong tea with honey specifically. FOODON_03301103 (tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0KL covers tea and infusion beverages broadly. No specific code exists for cassia seed oolong tea with honey.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance. Used as closest parent; no specific code for cassia seed oolong tea with honey exists.

main_ingredients: cassia seeds (Semen Cassiae / Jue Ming Zi)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of approximately 350 ml, as served by CHICHA San Chen in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA SR Legacy 14355) combined with Honey, raw (USDA SR Legacy 19304) and Cassia seed compositional data (FAO/INFOODS)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for cassia black tea with honey as a commercial beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) brewed black tea per 100 g (USDA FDC #14355, SR Legacy), (2) honey per 100 g (USDA FDC #19304, SR Legacy) at approximately 5 g honey per 100 ml beverage, and (3) cassia seed infusion contribution (minimal, per FAO/INFOODS compositional data for Semen Cassiae). The resulting blend is approximately 95% brewed tea and 5% honey by volume, yielding a lightly sweetened tea. Values represent the ready-to-drink beverage per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — honey and lightly sweetened tea analogues; Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cassia black tea with honey. GI estimated at 32 based on: (1) plain brewed black tea has a GI of approximately 0–1 (negligible carbohydrate); (2) honey has a published GI of approximately 58 (Atkinson et al. 2021, University of Sydney GI Database); (3) the beverage contains approximately 3.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from honey at ~5 g per 100 ml. Weighted blend yields an effective GI of approximately 32 for the ready-to-drink beverage. GL per 350 ml serving = (32 × 11.55 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.7, rounded to 3.8. gi_category is low (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: With only approximately 3 mg sodium per 100 g and no added salt, this lightly sweetened herbal tea poses minimal concern for individuals managing hypertension, though total daily fluid and sugar intake should still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived sugars contribute approximately 3.2 g sugar per 100 g; GI estimated at 32 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the glycemic index of this beverage is low (estimated GI 32), the honey content contributes free sugars (~11 g per 350 ml serving) that can affect postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake or request reduced honey, and account for this beverage within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; cassia seeds and oolong tea contain polyphenols with potential cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, and both oolong tea polyphenols and cassia seed anthraquinones have been associated with modest lipid-lowering and antioxidant effects in observational studies, making it a generally acceptable beverage for individuals with cardiovascular disease when consumed without excess honey.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cassia Black Tea with Honey (决明子红乌龙茶加百花香蜜) is a signature herbal-botanical tea beverage offered by CHICHA San Chen, a Taiwanese specialty tea chain with outlets in Singapore. It is made by brewing baked cassia seeds (Semen Cassiae, known in traditional Chinese medicine as Jue Ming Zi, 决明子) together with red oolong tea leaves, then sweetening the infusion with wildflower honey (百花香蜜). The baking process mellows the slightly bitter, nutty character of the cassia seeds and deepens the amber colour of the oolong base, producing a full-bodied, aromatic tea with a naturally sweet floral finish. Cassia seeds are widely used in traditional Chinese medicine and are believed to support liver health, improve vision, and have mild laxative and lipid-modulating properties, though clinical evidence remains mixed. The beverage is served chilled or at room temperature in a standard takeaway cup of approximately 350 ml. Nutritionally, it is a lightly sweetened tea providing approximately 13 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates (approximately 3.3 g per 100 g) derived almost entirely from honey-sourced sugars. Fat and saturated fat are negligible. Sodium is very low at approximately 3 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low GI), though the honey content contributes approximately 11 g of free sugars per serving, which is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The tea polyphenols from oolong and the anthraquinone compounds from cassia seeds may offer modest antioxidant and lipid-modulating benefits. This beverage is vegetarian, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but is not vegan due to the use of honey. It pairs well with milk-based desserts and mousse, as recommended by the restaurant.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry exists for this specific commercial beverage in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining USDA FDC SR Legacy data for brewed black tea (FDC #14355) and raw honey (FDC #19304) in proportions consistent with a lightly sweetened tea (~5 g honey per 100 ml). Cassia seed infusion contribution to macronutrients is minimal and consistent with FAO/INFOODS compositional data. GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as brewed tea and dilute honey solution contain no meaningful fat; confirmed zero per USDA FDC SR Legacy entries for brewed black tea and raw honey at this dilution. 2. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero for the same reason. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are 0.0: naturally zero for a brewed tea beverage with honey. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero; no hydrogenated fats present. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero for a filtered brewed tea beverage; cassia seed solids are not consumed. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero; no animal-derived fat present (honey contains no cholesterol). 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero; plant-based beverage with no animal-derived vitamin B12 source. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero; no dietary vitamin D source in this beverage. 9. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1.0: not reported in USDA FDC SR Legacy for brewed black tea or honey at this dilution; value not reliably estimable. 10. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 11. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 12. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 13. vitamin_b6_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 14. folate_mcg_dfe is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 15. vitamin_e_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 16. zinc_mg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 17. selenium_mcg is -1.0: not reported in the combined source data at this dilution level. 18. glycemic_index set to 32 (low GI) as an analogous estimate; gi_category low per convention. The beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates from the tea itself; all glycemic carbohydrates derive from honey. 19. Cassia seeds (Semen Cassiae) contain anthraquinone glycosides including emodin and aurantio-obtusin; long-term high-dose consumption has been associated with hepatotoxicity in case reports; this is noted for clinical context but does not affect the nutritional record. 20. The food_name in the input JSON ('Cassia Black Tea + Honey决明子红乌龙茶 + 百花香蜜') has been normalised to the canonical English form 'Cassia Black Tea with Honey'; the Chinese original is preserved in local_names and aliases. 21. This beverage is classified as Chinese cuisine per the input source; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been applied given the Singapore serving context.
